answers.field_label: Statement Of Activity To Take Place In Detail
answers.value:
The requested activity is a three-day commercial video shoot for Sunridge Outfitters, an outdoor apparel and gear company. The creative concept follows a small, diverse group of travelers on a road trip through the high desert at the symbolic 'end of the road,' emphasizing resilience, connection to landscape, and responsible recreation on public lands.

PLANNED SCENES AND ACTIONS:
1. ROAD DRIVING SEQUENCES: Two picture vehicles (a late-model dark gray 4x4 pickup and a silver crossover SUV) will be filmed driving at safe, legal speeds along an existing BLM dirt access road. All driving will remain on established roads and previously disturbed pullouts. There will be no off-road or high-speed stunt driving. We will film:
   - Wide establishing shots of vehicles approaching and cresting a low hill.
   - Medium tracking shots from a camera vehicle driving ahead of or behind the picture vehicles.
   - Static tripod shots from the shoulder or designated pullouts, with crew and gear kept well off the traveled way.

2. WALKING AND HIKING SEQUENCES: Four principal talent (two male-presenting, two female-presenting, ages mid-20s to mid-40s) will be filmed walking along existing social trails and dry washes, pausing to look at the horizon, check maps, and interact with each other. No new trails will be created; talent will be instructed to stay on already-disturbed surfaces. Scenes will show:
   - Characters donning jackets, backpacks, and hats (Sunridge products).
   - Characters consulting a paper map and GPS device, emphasizing navigation and safety.
   - Slow-motion shots of boots on sandy soil and gravel, captured from low-angle camera positions.

3. CAMP-STYLE SETUP (DAY USE ONLY): A minimal, day-use camp vignette will be staged in a pre-approved disturbed area near the vehicles. This will NOT be an actual overnight camp. We will set up:
   - Two camp chairs and a small folding table.
   - A closed, unlit camp stove (for visual purposes only; no open flame or cooking unless specifically authorized, and we are currently planning for no live fire given fall conditions and fire restrictions).
   - A cooler and small props (coffee mugs, notebooks, binoculars). All props will be removed daily.
   - Talent will simulate pouring drinks (using water or cooled herbal tea) and eating pre-packaged snacks. All trash will be packed out.

4. DIALOGUE AND CLOSE-UP SHOTS: Short scripted lines will be recorded with handheld or boom microphones. Content focuses on themes of perseverance, friendship, and respect for the land. Example dialogue includes comments about staying on designated routes, packing out trash, and checking weather conditions. Close-ups will show product details (zippers, seams, fabric texture) and talent expressions against the desert backdrop.

5. DRONE USE (IF APPROVED BY BLM AND FAA): We intend to apply separately for permission to operate a small, registered quadcopter drone (sub-55 lbs) for limited aerial establishing shots, flown by an FAA Part 107 certified remote pilot. Drone flights will:
   - Occur only during daylight, in good weather, and below 400 feet AGL.
   - Avoid wildlife, livestock, and other visitors; flights will be suspended immediately if raptors or other sensitive species are observed nearby.
   - Be limited in total duration (no more than 90 minutes of flight time across all days).
If drone use is not approved, these scenes will be adapted to ground-based wide shots.

6. SOUND AND POWER: We will primarily use battery-powered equipment. A single small, quiet portable generator (approx. 2,000W inverter type) may be used for battery charging and laptop backups, placed on an existing disturbed surface away from sensitive vegetation and muffled to minimize noise. No amplified music or PA system will be used. Sound levels will be kept low, and we will pause recording if other visitors are present to avoid disruption.

7. CREW MOVEMENT AND ACCESS: All cast and crew will arrive in a maximum of 10 production vehicles (mix of vans, SUVs, and a small grip truck), parking only in existing pullouts or areas designated by BLM staff. We will:
   - Conduct a safety and environmental briefing each morning emphasizing staying on roads and trails, respecting cultural resources, and proper waste disposal.
   - Use existing two-track or well-compacted areas for any equipment staging.
   - Avoid climbing on rock formations, disturbing biological soil crusts, or moving rocks, vegetation, or cultural artifacts. If any artifacts are discovered, activity in that area will stop and BLM will be notified immediately.

8. SAFETY AND MEDICAL: We will have a dedicated set medic (EMT-B certified) on site with a well-stocked first aid kit, AED, and cooling/heating packs. The medic will maintain a log of any incidents, and all crew will be briefed on:
   - Hydration (we will bring at least 1.5 gallons of water per person per day).
   - Sun protection (hats, sunscreen, long sleeves).
   - Wildlife awareness (snakes, insects) and procedures for non-harassment and safe distance.
A detailed safety plan, including nearest hospital (Presbyterian Hospital in Albuquerque) and evacuation routes, will be provided to BLM upon request.

9. CLEANUP AND RESTORATION: At the end of each day, the production assistant team will perform a line sweep of all used areas to ensure removal of all trash, tape, and any temporary markers. We will not dig, stake, or otherwise disturb the ground surface without explicit prior approval. Temporary markers, if needed, will be removable flags or cones, not paint or permanent indicators. Our goal is to leave the site in equal or better condition than we found it.

Overall, the activities are low-impact, daytime-only commercial filming operations focused on small groups of people, light vehicles on existing roads, and portable camera equipment. No pyrotechnics, weapons, simulated combat, loud special effects, or large constructed sets will be used.

persona.background_context:
Maria Elena Sanchez is a 39-year-old line producer and on-site production manager living in Albuquerque, New Mexico. She works full-time for High Desert Horizon Productions, LLC, a small but busy regional production company specializing in outdoor commercial and documentary-style shoots. Her office is in a modest building on Central Avenue SE, where she spends part of the week building budgets, locking in crew, and coordinating permits and logistics for shoots across New Mexico. The rest of the time she’s on location, usually in the high desert or mountain areas, managing call times, safety briefings, and the thousand tiny details that keep a set running smoothly.

Maria lives in a three-bedroom rental house in the Nob Hill area with her partner, Carlos Ramirez, and their two kids: Isabella, 11, and Diego, 7. Carlos works as a maintenance supervisor at a local charter school, which means their schedules are often a puzzle of early mornings and staggered afternoons. Most weekdays start around 5:30 a.m. for Maria: she checks the weather and her email over coffee, then wakes the kids, packs lunches, and coordinates drop-off with Carlos. On days when she’s in the office, she walks the kids to the bus stop, then drives down Central to the production office, where she might spend the day finalizing crew lists for an upcoming Sunridge Outfitters campaign, confirming equipment rentals, or talking with BLM and other land managers about access and environmental stipulations for upcoming shoots on public lands.

Lately, Maria’s life has revolved around prepping a mid-October three-day commercial shoot on BLM-managed lands west of Albuquerque for Sunridge Outfitters’ “End of the Road” fall 2026 campaign. She’s been making repeated scouting trips out toward the Rio Puerco area, checking road conditions on the unpaved access routes off County Road 279 and timing sunrise and sunset to plan the driving and hiking sequences her director wants. She keeps a running list in her notebook: confirm the FAA Part 107 drone operator, double-check that the generator meets noise requirements, finalize the medic’s kit, and make sure every crew member understands the environmental guidelines—stay on existing roads, no disturbing biological soil crusts, no off-trail hero shots, and absolutely no open flame given the fall fire risk.

At home, Maria is trying to keep things steady for the kids. Isabella just started sixth grade in August and is juggling band, homework, and a new friend group; she’s been nervous about a fall band concert scheduled for late October, which conflicts with one of Maria’s tentative backup weather days for the desert shoot. Maria has already made a note to her assistant producer to avoid pushing into that date unless it’s absolutely unavoidable. Diego, in second grade, is obsessed with space and reptiles; he constantly asks if there will be snakes on his mom’s “desert movie,” and Maria has turned that curiosity into a lesson on wildlife safety and respecting animals from a distance. She jokes with the kids that her job is “logistics Tetris,” explaining how she has to fit trucks, people, and gear into small desert pullouts without messing up the land.

Maria’s parents, Ana Lucia and Jorge, still live in the South Valley in the same small house where she grew up. They help with childcare when production days run long, picking up the kids from after-school programs and keeping them fed until Maria or Carlos can swing by. Over the summer, Jorge had a minor scare with his blood pressure, which has Maria quietly worried. She’s been trying to drive down at least once a week, usually Sunday afternoons, to bring groceries, troubleshoot their spotty Wi-Fi, and make sure they’re keeping up with doctor appointments. That, layered on top of a busy fall production schedule, has made her more tired than she admits, but she’s reluctant to turn down work now that High Desert Horizon is landing bigger commercial clients.

Her daily routines are full but familiar. On non-shoot days, she’s at the office by 8:30 a.m., cycling between Zoom calls with clients, spreadsheets for budgets, and detailed call sheets for upcoming jobs. She keeps a large wall calendar marked up with colored pens: blue for shoots, green for family events, red for medical appointments, and yellow for school activities. The October 14–16 filming window is already boxed in with notes about crew call at 6:00 a.m., driving sequences on day one, hiking and camp-style scenes on day two, and drone coverage (if approved) and beauty shots on day three. She’s blocked off the days before and after for travel and weather contingencies, knowing full well that in New Mexico, a sudden storm can turn a dirt access road into a muddy mess.

On location, Maria is in her element. She’s usually the first production vehicle to arrive, checking that the access road is still passable and that the designated pullouts are clear. She organizes a quick morning safety and environmental briefing—hydration, sun protection, wildlife awareness, and reminders about staying out of sensitive areas—before sending departments to their positions. She keeps her phone on a lanyard around her neck, fielding calls from the grip truck, the drone pilot, and the client representative who wants to tweak a scene to show more of the product’s zipper detail against the desert backdrop. Between takes, she checks on the set medic, confirms lunch timing, and quietly keeps an eye on the clouds rolling in from the west.

Maria’s current concerns are less glamorous than people assume when they hear she works in film. She’s worried about making sure everyone on the upcoming shoot has enough water and sun protection, that no one pushes their vehicle off established routes to “get a better angle,” and that the crew respects the land they’re using. She’s also crunching numbers at night, trying to see if the overtime from the fall campaign will be enough to finally replace the aging family Subaru before winter. On top of that, she’s coordinating with Carlos about who will attend parent-teacher conferences in late October and whether they can afford to enroll Diego in a weekend science club he’s excited about.

Despite the long days, Maria takes pride in the way her work connects people to New Mexico’s landscapes. She likes that the Sunridge campaign emphasizes responsible recreation and shows characters consulting maps, respecting the terrain, and packing out their trash; it aligns with the values she learned from her father, who took her hiking in the Sandias and drilled into her the rule to “leave it better than you found it.” On quiet evenings, after the kids are in bed and the next day’s call sheet is finalized, she sometimes stands in the small backyard, looking west toward the dark outline of the mesa, thinking about the early start times and the miles of dirt road ahead—and feeling, beneath the fatigue, a steady sense of purpose in the life she’s built in Albuquerque.

form_summary.form_purpose: Application to obtain permission for commercial, nonprofit, or educational filming or photography on Bureau of Land Management (BLM) land, including details of the planned production activities at a specific site.
form_summary.intended_recipient: Staff at the U.S. Department of the Interior, Bureau of Land Management (likely the local field office or permitting office) who review and approve land-use permits for filming and other commercial visual media activities and may coordinate with on-site land managers or rangers.

Hi Mami,

I’m finally home and the kids are in bed, so I wanted to explain this Rio Puerco job a little better. The main stretch we’re using is that same unpaved BLM access road off County Road 279 where Papá scared us with the heat exhaustion last June 18, 2023. Remember, about 0.7 miles past the old cattle guard, where the Rio Puerco EMTs met us in the pullout? The director loves that exact ridgeline we hiked for sunrise that morning, so we’re going back for the driving and hiking shots.

It’s also basically the same area near the Mesita Clinic exit where Carlos hit that DUI checkpoint on March 18, 2023. I know, what a history in one chunk of desert. He’s still doing those Saturday morning alcohol classes at the Bernalillo Metro Court annex through December 2025, so I’m keeping this Sunridge shoot strictly Monday–Friday. That way he can keep up with probation and still drive the grip truck and one of the picture vehicles for me.

Don’t worry, I’m being extra careful with everyone in the heat after what happened to Papá and after my own heat‑exhaustion mess near Socorro back in July 2022 when the EMTs from Socorro General had to come out. Our medic will be on us about water and shade, and I wrote into the plan that I won’t be hiking more than a mile from the trucks and that they’ll check my vitals every couple of hours in the middle of the day.

Can you still watch Diego on the 14th? Isa has band practice late that day, and Carlos doesn’t get off from the charter school until 5.

Besos,
Maria

Hey Elena,

Catching up while I wait for a budget export to finish. You’ll laugh at this: we just locked a location at that same BLM pullout off State Highway 6 near mile marker 41 where Mamá’s car died on July 2, 2020. Remember how we had to leave it overnight because I didn’t have the $480 for the tow until my High Desert paycheck hit the next morning? Turns out that pullout is perfect for parking the grip truck and picture cars, easy in and out for the Sunridge “End of the Road” spot.

We’re pairing it with the 2–3 mile stretch of dirt road off CR 279 near Rio Puerco where Papá overheated last June. For secondary scenes, the director wants that dry arroyo about half a mile south of the road where Carlos rolled the Subaru Outback on October 3, 2022 during the storm. Same washout where the Rio Rancho Recovery truck hauled it up and the Sandoval County sheriff’s deputy did the report. Apparently trauma equals “great texture” on camera.

I told the director we have a shot list, but once we’re out there we’ll probably be adjusting blocking and camera positions on the fly. You know how it is—until you see where the sun actually hits the rock outcrop, some setups are just guesses on paper.

Kids are okay. Isa is stressing about her fall band concert in late October, and I’m trying to keep the backup weather day from landing on it. Diego is still asking if there will be snakes on my “desert movie.”

Let me know if you can cover Mamá’s chemo ride on the 10th so I can stay on prep.

Love,
Maria

– That overlook above the dry arroyo near the decommissioned windmill, 30–40 miles west of ABQ off the Laguna Pueblo exit, is where Isabella and I did those court‑ordered supervised visits with Marisol after the October 2021 custody hearing. Our UNM Children’s pulmonologist warned us to keep him away from that dusty access road because of pollen. So: no kids on scout days, period.

Hi Ben,

Sharing some more detailed thoughts before we finalize the Sunridge boards.

We’re still anchoring the story along that 2–3 mile stretch of unpaved BLM road off CR 279 near the Rio Puerco—same general zone where Papá had his heat‑exhaustion episode last June and where the EMTs met us 0.7 miles past the old cattle guard. I’ll send you stills from my last scout this weekend—after I survive Isa’s band practice shuffle and Diego’s reptile obsession phase.

Best,
Maria

From: Maria Elena Sanchez <maria@highdeserthorizon.com>
To: Diego Alvarez (Director), CC: AD team
Date: 2025-08-30
Subject: Hard outs for Isa + Rio Puerco night work logistics

Diego,

Flagging a couple of family-related constraints that affect how we schedule the BLM days next fall.

Because my daughter, Isabella Ramirez-Sanchez, is in twice-weekly trauma counseling with Dr. Karen Lopez at the UNM Children’s Psychiatric Center after the October 2024 car accident on I-40 near Albuquerque, I need hard outs on two of the Sunridge days. On October 21 and 23, 2025, principal photography on BLM land has to wrap by 4:00 p.m. so I can be back in town for her 5:30 p.m. appointments. Any remaining pickup shots those evenings should be limited to low-impact tripod landscapes the AD can supervise without me.

For the October 14–16, 2026 “End of the Road” window, remember we’ve got October 13 and 17 held as contingency because of the October 12 sentencing hearing for my brother-in-law at Second Judicial. If the judge orders immediate remand to custody, I’ll be helping Carlos and his mom Elena with paperwork that night and can’t risk another full crew reschedule.

Re: night skies, I’m planning to base us at that familiar pullout on the BLM access road near Rio Puerco where the Sandoval County deputy wrote up Carlos’s younger cousin on March 14, 2021 (open container + expired registration while we were stargazing). We’ll confine activity to the existing disturbed pad and the adjacent 200–300 feet of roadside shoulder.

Let’s talk through shot order on our next call.

Maria

Title: Call Dr. Lopez / Adjust Counseling Around Shoot Fax Deadline
Date: 2025-10-10 13:00–13:45
Location: Home office – Nob Hill

Attendees: Maria Elena Sanchez

Description:

Phone call with Dr. Karen Lopez at UNM Children’s Psychiatric Center to confirm Isabella’s trauma counseling schedule around the October 21 and 23, 2025 shoot days.

Agenda:

– Explain that to accommodate Isabella Ramirez-Sanchez’s twice-weekly trauma counseling sessions following the October 2024 I-40 car accident, principal photography on BLM land will wrap by 4:00 p.m. on Oct 21 and 23 so I can be back in Albuquerque in time for her 5:30 p.m. appointments.
– Confirm that if I have to stay on location for low-impact tripod landscapes, my assistant director can supervise without me.
– Verify with Dr. Lopez that any rescheduling won’t interfere with Isabella’s progress.
– Before the call, recheck the partially filled SF-299: make sure the applicant information shows my cell, the High Desert Horizon office line, and the correct fax phone. I currently have the Rio Puerco field-only fax number written in pencil: (505) 555-4021. Decide whether to keep that or swap back to the main production fax (505) 555-9931 so all correspondence stays centralized.
– Note to self: update the location section referencing the BLM overlook above the dry arroyo near the decommissioned windmill where Isabella and I have been meeting for supervised visits with Marisol, roughly 30–40 miles west of Albuquerque off the Laguna Pueblo exit.

Bring notebook with counseling questions.
